subsections.title: Microservices Fundamentals
subsections.keyRule: Microservices = each service owns one business capability, its own database, deploys independently. Trade-offs vs monolith: + independent scaling, + independent deployment, + tech diversity. - network calls instead of in-memory, - distributed transactions complexity, - operational overhead.

terms.what: Microservices architecture decomposes an application into small, independent services — each responsible for one business capability, deployable and scalable independently.
terms.why: Large monoliths are hard to scale, deploy, and maintain. Microservices allow each part to scale independently and be owned by a focused team.
terms.how: Each service: its own codebase, its own DB, REST or event-based communication, independently deployed as Docker container, orchestrated by Kubernetes.
terms.where: Large products with multiple teams: e-commerce (order service, inventory service, payment service, user service).
terms.when: Use microservices when: multiple teams working on the same app, need to scale different parts independently, different services have different tech needs. Avoid for small teams or simple apps — monolith is simpler.
terms.issue: Microservices introduce distributed systems problems: network failures, partial failures, eventual consistency, distributed tracing complexity.
terms.fix: Design for failure: circuit breakers, retry, timeouts on every service call. Use correlation IDs for distributed tracing. Use saga pattern for distributed transactions.

star.s: Interview: when would you NOT use microservices?
star.a: Small team (<5 devs), simple domain, tight coupling between features, early-stage product. Monolith first, extract services when pain points are clear.

terms.what: REST = HTTP/JSON — human readable, widely supported. gRPC = HTTP/2 with Protobuf — binary, typed, 5-10x faster, code-generated clients.
terms.why: REST is simpler, universal, works with any HTTP client. gRPC is faster and type-safe for high-throughput internal service calls.
terms.how: REST: Spring WebClient or Feign client for HTTP/JSON. gRPC: define service in .proto file, generate client+server code, call like a local method.
terms.where: REST for external APIs. gRPC for high-performance internal service-to-service calls.
terms.when: Use REST for public APIs and external-facing. Use gRPC for internal microservice communication needing high throughput (1000+ calls/sec).
terms.issue: REST with JSON serialization overhead adds up for high-frequency internal calls. Protobuf is 3-5x smaller and 2-10x faster to serialize.
terms.fix: For performance-critical internal services, migrate to gRPC. Keep REST for external-facing APIs.

star.s: Order service calls 5 other services per request — 200ms overhead just in HTTP/JSON.
star.r: Inter-service call time dropped from 200ms to 40ms.

terms.what: Service Discovery lets services find each other by name instead of hardcoded IP/port. Registry keeps list of healthy service instances.
terms.why: In Kubernetes/cloud, services start/stop, scale up/down — IPs change constantly. Service discovery handles this dynamically.
terms.how: Each service registers with Eureka on startup. Clients ask Eureka for 'order-service' — get list of healthy instances. Ribbon/Spring LoadBalancer picks one.
terms.where: Any microservices deployment needing service-to-service communication. In Kubernetes, kube-dns provides similar functionality natively.
terms.when: Use Eureka for Spring Cloud deployments. In Kubernetes, rely on Kubernetes DNS — no Eureka needed.

terms.what: API Gateway is the single entry point for all client requests. It routes to the right microservice, handles auth, rate limiting, SSL, and logging centrally.
terms.why: Without a gateway, clients must know about all services, all do auth separately, CORS must be configured in each. Gateway centralizes all of this.
terms.how: Spring Cloud Gateway or Kong. Routes: /users/* → user-service, /orders/* → order- service. Add filters for: JWT validation, rate limiting, request logging.
terms.where: Every microservices deployment that has external clients (web, mobile, third party).
terms.when: Always have an API Gateway for external access. Do NOT use it for internal service-to-service calls — adds unnecessary latency.
terms.issue: API Gateway becomes a bottleneck or single point of failure if not highly available.
terms.fix: Deploy gateway in multiple instances behind a load balancer. Use async processing and avoid stateful operations in the gateway.

star.s: Mobile app must call 5 services for one screen — multiple network hops.
star.a: Added API Gateway aggregator endpoint that calls 5 services and returns combined response.
star.r: Mobile app makes 1 call instead of 5. Simpler and faster.

terms.what: Circuit breaker monitors calls to a service. If failure rate exceeds a threshold, it 'opens' — subsequent calls fail immediately without trying the service. Prevents cascade failures.
terms.why: If Service B is down and Service A keeps calling it, A's threads fill up waiting for timeouts — A goes down too. Circuit breaker prevents this chain reaction.
terms.how: CLOSED (normal) → too many failures → OPEN (fail fast) → after timeout → HALF- OPEN (test one call) → success → CLOSED. Configure: failureRateThreshold, waitDurationInOpenState.
terms.where: Every service-to-service HTTP/gRPC call in microservices. Spring Cloud Resilience4j @CircuitBreaker annotation.
terms.when: Always add circuit breakers for external service calls. Also add fallback methods to return cached or default data when circuit is open.
terms.issue: Circuit breaker with no fallback = user gets error when circuit opens. Better than cascade failure but still bad UX.
terms.fix: Add @CircuitBreaker(fallbackMethod='getFallback'). Fallback returns: cached data, empty list, degraded response. User sees something useful instead of error.

star.s: Recommendation service down takes down product page too.
star.a: @CircuitBreaker on recommendation call with fallback returning empty list. Product page works, no recommendations shown.

terms.what: Distributed tracing tracks a single request as it flows through multiple microservices. Each service adds trace context — all logs linked by one trace ID.
terms.why: In microservices, one user request might touch 10 services. Without tracing, finding which service caused a failure or slowness is almost impossible.
terms.how: Spring Cloud Sleuth adds trace ID and span ID to every request and log line. Zipkin or Jaeger collects and visualizes traces. See full request journey in one dashboard.
terms.where: Every microservices deployment. Critical for debugging performance issues and failures.
terms.when: Add distributed tracing from day one — impossible to debug microservices without it.
terms.issue: Not propagating trace headers when making async calls or using non- instrumented HTTP clients.

terms.what: Sidecar deploys a helper container alongside the main service container in the same pod. Handles cross-cutting concerns: logging, config, proxy, service mesh communication.
terms.why: Moves infrastructure concerns (logging agent, config refresh, mTLS termination) out of the service code into a separate container.
terms.how: Main container: your Java service. Sidecar: Envoy proxy (Istio), Fluentd log shipper, config reload agent. They share network namespace — sidecar sees all traffic.
terms.where: Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd), log aggregation, config management, mTLS between services.
terms.when: Use sidecar when infrastructure concerns should not be coded into the service itself. Common in Kubernetes-based deployments.
terms.issue: Sidecar adds resource overhead — extra memory and CPU per pod. More containers = more complexity.
terms.fix: Use sidecar only for genuine cross-cutting infrastructure. Avoid sidecar for business logic.

star.s: 20 microservices need mTLS, distributed tracing, and metrics collection.
star.a: Istio sidecar injection. Envoy handles mTLS, tracing headers.
star.r: All 20 services secured and traced with zero code changes.

terms.what: Bulkhead isolates resources per downstream service — separate thread pools for each. If one service is slow, only its thread pool fills up, not the whole server.
terms.why: Without bulkhead: slow external service consumes all threads → entire application unresponsive. Bulkhead limits blast radius.
terms.how: Resilience4j @Bulkhead(name='paymentService', type=THREADPOOL): separate thread pool per downstream. Or SEMAPHORE type: limit concurrent calls.
terms.where: Services calling multiple external dependencies (payment, inventory, shipping). Each gets its own thread pool.
terms.when: Use when a service calls multiple external services and one failing/slow service should not cascade to others.
terms.issue: Thread pool bulkhead increases total thread count — careful with memory sizing.
terms.fix: Size each bulkhead's thread pool based on expected concurrency and SLA for that service.

star.s: Slow inventory service makes entire order service unresponsive — all threads waiting.
star.a: Bulkhead with separate thread pool for inventory.
star.r: Inventory slowness limited to its pool. Order service serves other requests normally.

terms.what: Retry automatically retries failed operations a specified number of times before giving up. With backoff: wait increases between retries to reduce load on struggling service.
terms.why: Transient failures (network hiccup, brief service restart) resolve quickly — retry saves the user from seeing an error.
terms.where: HTTP calls to external services, database operations, message processing.
terms.when: Use retry for transient failures. Do NOT retry non-idempotent operations without checking first (don't retry a payment twice).
terms.issue: Retrying non-idempotent calls (POST, payment charge) causes duplicate operations.
terms.fix: Only retry safe/idempotent operations. Use idempotency keys for non-idempotent operations. Don't retry on 4xx errors.

star.s: 5% of external API calls fail due to transient issues — user sees errors.
star.a: @Retry with 3 attempts, exponential backoff. Fallback to cached data if all fail.
star.r: 99.9% success rate. Transient failures invisible to users.

terms.what: Rate limiting controls how many requests can be made in a time window — limits excessive calls to prevent overload or API quota exhaustion.
terms.why: External APIs have rate limits. Internal services can be protected from traffic bursts. Prevent API abuse.
terms.how: Resilience4j @RateLimiter(name='apiLimiter'): limitForPeriod=10, limitRefreshPeriod=1s. Or API Gateway level: max 100 req/sec per IP.
terms.where: API Gateway (limit per client), service-to-service calls (stay within third-party API quota), public REST endpoints.
terms.when: Use rate limiting to protect services from being overwhelmed and to respect external API limits.
terms.issue: Rate limit too strict causes legitimate requests to be rejected. Too loose gives no protection.
terms.fix: Set limits based on measured capacity and SLA. Implement graceful degradation when rate limit hit — serve cached or simplified response.

star.s: Third-party payment API quota: 100 calls/sec. Under load we send 500/sec — get throttled.
star.r: Never exceed quota. Under-load queue drains.

terms.what: Feign Client: declarative HTTP client — define interface + annotations, Spring generates the implementation. RestTemplate: blocking HTTP client (deprecated in favor of WebClient). WebClient: reactive non-blocking HTTP client.
terms.why: Feign removes boilerplate for service-to-service calls. WebClient is the modern default for Spring. RestTemplate is still common in legacy code.
terms.how: @FeignClient(name='user-service', url='${user.service.url}') interface UserClient { @GetMapping('/users/{id}') User findUser(@PathVariable Long id); }
terms.where: Microservice-to-microservice communication. Feign + Ribbon/LoadBalancer for client-side load balancing.
terms.when: New code: WebClient for async. Feign Client for simple synchronous service calls. Avoid new RestTemplate — use WebClient or Feign instead.
terms.issue: Feign Client doesn't propagate security headers by default — auth token missing on downstream calls.
terms.fix: Add Feign RequestInterceptor to copy Authorization header from SecurityContextHolder to outgoing requests.

star.s: Service needs to call 3 other services — RestTemplate boilerplate.
star.a: Three @FeignClient interfaces. Service calls them like local methods.

terms.what: Saga: distributed transaction split into local transactions with compensating actions. Choreography: services emit events, react to each other — decentralized. Orchestration: central coordinator tells each service what to do.
terms.why: No two-phase commit across microservices. Saga achieves eventual consistency through compensating transactions on failure.
terms.how: Choreography: OrderService emits OrderCreated → PaymentService charges → emits PaymentDone → InventoryService reserves. Orchestration: OrderSaga orchestrator calls each service in sequence, calls compensation on failure.
terms.where: Order processing, hotel/flight booking, e-commerce checkout with multiple services.
terms.when: Use Choreography for simple linear flows. Use Orchestration for complex flows where visibility and central control matter.
terms.issue: Choreography: hard to trace flow across many events. Orchestration: orchestrator becomes a bottleneck.
terms.fix: Use Saga orchestration with Axon or Spring State Machine for visibility. Add correlation IDs for trace.

star.s: Order requires payment + inventory + shipping across 3 microservices.
star.a: Saga orchestration. If inventory fails: compensate by refunding payment.
star.r: Consistent state even on partial failure.

terms.what: Outbox pattern: write both the DB change AND an event to an 'outbox' table in the same transaction. A separate poller publishes events from outbox to message broker.
terms.why: Without outbox: save to DB, then publish to Kafka — if Kafka publish fails, DB saved but event lost. Outbox ensures both succeed or both fail atomically.
terms.how: BEGIN TRANSACTION: INSERT into orders(data); INSERT into outbox(event_type, payload); COMMIT; Poller reads outbox, publishes to Kafka, deletes outbox row.
terms.where: Any service that must both save data AND publish events reliably (exactly-once guarantee).
terms.when: Use outbox when DB write and event publication must be atomic — i.e., never have data without event or event without data.
terms.issue: Outbox poller adds latency — event published seconds after DB write, not milliseconds.
terms.fix: For low-latency, use Debezium (change data capture) instead of polling — reads DB transaction log directly.

star.s: Orders saved but order-created events sometimes not published — downstream services miss orders.
star.a: Outbox pattern. Event written transactionally with order. Debezium publishes from transaction log.
